"The fuel everyone thinks is abundant is not."

Matthew's letter shows storage capacity grew 7% since 2010 while demand grew 54%, with another 39% coming by 2030. 

He forecasts an unprecedented gas shortage starting in 2028, and explains why the market won't see it coming:

"Natural gas is well supplied today, '26 and '27, and the result is that nobody is investing in gas. 

EQT is shutting in natural gas right now because they think it'll be more valuable later.

The things that we can see real time to figure out if the market is onto this tightness — no one's onto it. 

It's perpetuating this view that most Americans have, which is there's plenty of natural gas, because for 15 years it's all we've been told.

There's a complacency that's developed, and we think that complacency is gonna take us right up to the point where it's too late. 

Gas has lulled everybody to sleep.

In '28, the curve is flat because people believe the gas is abundant. That's despite all these AI compute announcements.

We start to draw meaningfully in the middle of '28 on the gas system like we've never drawn before. By 2029, we drop below all known historical storage evidence.

Prices have gone to $6 or $8 or $10, but those have been transitory. 

What we're talking about are structural drivers of demand against a known possible production of gas, and they don't match up.

The deficit gets really convex and unbounded."

tweets.text:
Matthew Smith has spent the last 18 months modeling every well, pipeline, storage facility, and power plant in the American natural gas system.

His conclusion is that the US is heading toward a natural gas shortage with no precedent, beginning in 2028.

By 2030, he believes we could exhaust our working natural gas storage entirely.

The fuel everyone in AI is counting on, and that everyone assumes is abundant, is not there.

And because gas sets the price of electricity in most of the country, he argues Americans will pay for the shortage in their power bills.

Matthew has worked in energy markets for over 20 years and is the CIO of Chronometer Partners. 

This is his second time on the show, and he's one of my favorite people to talk to about energy.

We discuss:
- Why the bottleneck is moving from power to fuel
- Why we can't just shut off exports
- 2028 as the inflection point
- Large-scale nuclear v. SMRs
- Who wins, who loses, and what can still be done

Enjoy!

TIMESTAMPS
0:00 Intro
1:30 What Drives the Deficit
11:00 Why Supply Can’t Catch Up
20:35 The 2030 Gas Crisis
25:05 Winners and Losers
29:00 Nuclear and Solar
33:30 Consumers Pay the Bill
37:20 AI’s Next Shortage
45:25 Solutions and Global Stakes
51:15 The Coming Gas Knife Fight

tweets.text:
Not long ago, @IOHK_Charles called the @realfi_co testnet a "big TVL and TX generator for Cardano."

RealFi's own pitch reaches further: give people access to financial services without leaning on traditional banks. That gap between what Charles claimed and what RealFi promises is what pulled me in.

I've been sleeping on Cardano for a while, so I decided to dig into this properly.

1️⃣ What is RealFi?

RealFi is building a stablecoin ecosystem on Cardano designed to connect on-chain capital to real-world credit markets.
Its main stablecoin, USDr, is designed to be backed by real-world assets such as tokenized US Treasuries, money-market funds, public credit, and direct loans to businesses.

2️⃣ What does it do?

RealFi plans to spread the capital backing USDr across liquid financial assets and different forms of credit. Their direct loans can run around 6 to 24 months and focus on companies in payments, digital lending, asset financing, and renewable energy.

So instead of leaning on token emissions or crypto trading strategies, the goal is to generate returns from traditional financial assets and real borrowers.

Users can hold USDr as the liquid stablecoin or stake it to receive sUSDr and earn part of the income generated by that portfolio.

3️⃣ The two-token structure

USDr stays the liquid side and holds close to $1, while sUSDr is the return-bearing side. RealFi's site currently lists up to 10% APY for sUSDr.

If the underlying portfolio earns, that income flows into sUSDr through weekly updates, so each sUSDr should gradually become redeemable for more USDr.

sUSDr also carries more of the risk. The design puts the risk on sUSDr in exchange for the returns, while USDr is meant to stay the stable, liquid side. The exact loss mechanics are worth reading straight from RealFi's own materials before you commit anything.

The way I think about it is simple: someone who mainly wants liquidity stays in USDr, while someone willing to take on more credit risk for higher returns moves into sUSDr.

RealFi also plans to keep around 10–20% of the portfolio in assets that convert to cash quickly, such as tokenized Treasuries and money-market funds.

That gives them a pool for withdrawals, while the rest gets deployed into higher-return assets like public and private credit. So they avoid putting every dollar into longer-term loans and hoping nobody wants to exit.

The two-token idea itself isn't unique, but the way RealFi pairs a liquid stablecoin with a return-bearing layer for a mixed RWA and private-credit portfolio is the part that stands out to me.

One thing worth repeating: private credit carries real credit risk, any committed capital is at risk, and RealFi is unavailable in the US, UK, and EU/EEA.

4️⃣ The testnet

Before real assets and borrower repayments enter the system, RealFi needs to test whether users can move through the full product flow without something breaking. That's where the new testnet comes in.

Phase 1 stress-tests the app, its flows, and integrations, and it's already pulling traffic:
 → 1k+ users joined
 → ~500 active, verified wallets in Phase 1
 → 2k+ new followers here on X
 → 420+ new joiners on Discord

Retail testers can swap test USDCx into USDr, stake USDr for sUSDr, then unstake it again. KYC-verified institutional partners separately test the mint and redemption process for moving larger amounts in and out.

Users also earn R-Points for helping test these flows. R-Points track participation, while RealFi has positioned the future RFG token as governance, meant to give participants a voice in key protocol decisions.

So the flywheel is clear: R-Points drive participation, users generate more transactions, and those transactions give RealFi more chances to catch failed flows before mainnet.

If you'd like to experience it first hand, test it out, here: https://t.co/nOixMX2AsZ

5️⃣ Closing thoughts

I read the two-token structure mainly as a way to separate liquidity from risk. The testnet can show whether that design holds at the product level, but the bigger question is whether RealFi can connect it to real loans and actual economic activity.

That's the real link between the stablecoin and Cardano's old "bank the unbanked" idea.

So I wouldn't say Cardano has delivered on the whole promise yet, but RealFi is at least building something that could move it closer.

Views are my own, none of this is financial advice. Do your own research.

Fanatics Fest last week had 200k attendees, and hardly any of them have ever even heard of onchain TCG platforms.

San Diego Comic Con this week is expecting similar numbers.

I think crypto investors, who often can't see beyond our tiny crypto echo chamber, forget just how massive the collectibles market really is. There are hundreds of millions of collectors out there actively collecting and genuinely passionate about this vertical.

With just 1-2k daily active users (and most of them coming from crypto) platforms like Collector Crypt and Beezie haven't even properly started tapping into these markets yet, and they're already among the highest revenue generating projects in all of crypto.

And the good news: onboarding these people is only a matter of time. Collecting onchain gives them a user experience orders of magnitude better than what they're used to today.

Give it time. But the Gacha platforms that make the right moves now and use their resources to win over non-crypto users at scale have genuine decacorn potential from here.

tweets.text:
Most financial products make you pick one:

Lock your asset for yield, or keep it liquid and earn nothing.

Bitcoin has basically lived with that trade-off for years. 

Most holders would rather sit on idle BTC than hand it over just to squeeze out a bit more yield.

Last week, @StackingDao announced stBTC.

The idea is pretty straightforward. BTC earns an expected base yield of around 2.6%, while the stBTC representing that position stays liquid enough to be used across lending and trading apps on Stacks.

BitGo has now added the sBTC bridge, so it’s clients can move between BTC and sBTC without changing the custody platform they’re already using.

BitGo secures around $63B in assets across 5,569 clients and 1.2 million users. That’s a fairly meaningful piece of infrastructure joining the ecosystem.

What I like is how the pieces are starting to connect.

Native BTC yield, a liquid version through stBTC, and custody providers beginning to support the flow instead of sitting outside it.

There are still execution and smart-contract risks once these assets start moving through DeFi.

But if Bitcoin ever develops a real onchain economy, this is probably what it looks like at the beginning.

Pleased to partner with @Stacks to highlight this one.

BitGo has integrated the sBTC bridge.
Institutions can now convert BTC to sBTC and back directly within @BitGo.

tweets.text:
Bitcoin hasn't fought over its core rules like this since the Blocksize Wars.

There are 2 fights happening at once. 

One's over jpegs and the other's over quantum computers.

Here’s what’s going on:

#1 - BIP-110

This is mainly aimed at Ordinals, Runes, and other data people store inside Bitcoin transactions.

The proposal would temporarily block some of the methods used to jam large amounts of data into blocks. Your $BTC stays safe, existing Ordinals stay where they are, but minting new inscriptions gets way harder for roughly a year.

Here’s the case for it.

Blockspace is limited. So every full node has to download and verify whatever gets added, and supporters think jpegs and tokens are making Bitcoin more expensive to use and harder to run over time.

Where it gets messy is Bitcoin has no idea WHAT the data is. It only sees how the transaction was built.

The same transaction structures used for jpegs today could power better wallets, custody systems, contracts, or financial applications tomorrow.

So the real fight is should Bitcoin change its core rules to block a use case that already follows the rules and pays fees?

The rollout has problems of its own.

Back in February, a Bitcoin Core dev found a consensus bug in the activation software. A running node could accept a block that a freshly synced BIP-110 node would reject, leaving them on different versions of Bitcoin's history.

It got patched, and a later audit called the fix clean. But it shows why consensus changes need far more testing than a normal software update.

As of late June, 0.31% of hashrate was signaling support. The threshold is 55%. F2Pool has publicly refused, and no other major pool has joined.

The real deadline is early August. Around block 961,632, mandatory signaling kicks in and BIP-110 nodes start rejecting every block that doesn't signal support. At current levels, that's nearly every block.

I’m watching  whether those nodes end up on their own minority chain or their operators quietly back down.

IMO cutting useless data is a fair goal. But a consensus change is the strongest tool Bitcoin has, and without clear evidence of serious harm, the fix creates more risk than the problem it solves.

#2 - BIP-361

This is an early plan for protecting Bitcoin from future quantum computers.

It would introduce safer wallet types, give users roughly five years to move their $BTC, and later stop old vulnerable addresses from receiving or spending coins.

Nothing changes today bc quantum computers can't currently break Bitcoin.

But wallet migrations take years, and Bitcoin moves slowly by design.

The point is to lock the rules in before the threat becomes real, especially for old wallets whose public keys are already visible and for dormant coins whose owners may never return.

There are two diff fights happening but the question is how high should the bar be for changing Bitcoin's rules?

tweets.text:
A new type of "high-level programming language" that seems really worth trying to make, is a language that gets compiled to Lean (or HOL, or...) that is specifically about making it as friendly as possible for a human to read definitions and theorems.

Not the proofs - as all that matters with proofs is that the proofs are correct - just the definitions and theorems.

The intended use case is that AI outputs a blob of proofs, and you're trying to make it as easy as possible for anyone reading the output to understand what the actual precise claims are that have been proven

$ZIM: Trading With Handcuffs On

Every day $ZIM buyout process continues, shareholder value is not being fully maximized.

Not because the business is weak. Quite the opposite.

The current freight market has turned $ZIM into a money-printing machine. Yet management remains constrained while the buyout process is unresolved.

A company that could be returning more capital to shareholders, paying special dividends, optimizing its fleet, and monetizing non-core assets is instead limited by the ongoing process.

Take Matson ( $MATX ) as an example.

Matson is widely considered one of the highest-quality shipping companies in the industry. It benefits from the protected Jones Act, which limits foreign competition on domestic U.S. shipping routes, giving it stable earnings and premium valuation multiples.

It is also currently aggressively buying back its own stock, something $ZIM cannot do as long as the buyout process drags on.

https://t.co/zCkIOhgItE

Now let’s compare the two companies.

$MATX
• Market cap: ~$6.7B
• Cash: ~$119M
• Fleet: ~68,000 TEU
• Expected annual earnings: ~$400M

$ZIM
• Market cap: ~$3.0B
• Cash today: ~$2.6B
• Our estimates: ~$3.0B cash by the end of Q2 and ~$3.8 by Q4.
• Fleet: ~720,000 TEU, growing toward ~900,000 TEU
• Expected annual earnings: ~$700M–1.5B

Think about that for a moment.

The market values $MATX at more than twice the market capitalization of $ZIM.

Yet $ZIM has:
More than 10x the fleet capacity.
Cash equal to almost its entire market capitalization today.
The potential, in our view, to earn 2–4x as much as $MATX under the current freight environment.

Matson deserves a premium. It is an outstanding business.

But does it deserve to be worth more than twice a company that is dramatically larger, has billions more in cash, and could generate substantially higher earnings?

We don’t think so.

One final point.

In our view, the probability of $ZIM ultimately being sold, whether to an Israeli buyer or a foreign buyer, is close to zero.

Yet the ongoing buyout process continues to prevent management from fully maximizing shareholder value through capital allocation decisions such as special dividends, fleet optimization, and strategic asset sales.

The sooner this overhang is removed, the sooner management can focus entirely on unlocking the value that we believe is already there.

One important clarification: this is a long-term investment. If you’re looking for a quick 20% gain over the next few months, this is probably not the right stock.

In fact, the share price could still decline when the merger is officially terminated. There may still be investors holding on to the belief that the deal could somehow go through, even though we believe the market has largely recognized that the acquisition is no longer likely.

Our investment horizon is at least two years. As we mentioned before, we believe investors could receive at least a 50% total return from dividends alone over that period, assuming our outlook proves correct. The amount of capital appreciation is much harder to estimate, so we won’t speculate on it.

This is not a momentum trade. It is a long-term investment based on what we believe is a significant disconnect between the company’s intrinsic value and its current market price.

Why Is $STAA Falling?

In my opinion, the recent weakness has little to do with the company’s underlying business.

The company preannounced an excellent quarter, with expected Q2 revenue of more than $90 million versus $44.3 million a year ago. China continued to recover, inventory levels normalized, and the Americas posted double digit growth.

So why is the stock down?

I think there are three reasons.

1. Higher interest rate fears.

Growth stocks have been under pressure as investors worry that interest rates could stay higher for longer.

2. Management’s cautious comments.

Although the preliminary results were strong, management warned that the Middle East and other regions affected by war, geopolitical instability, and economic uncertainty could continue to weigh on results.

3. Concerns about China competition.

Wedbush hosted an advisor call with a former STAAR Surgical Senior Vice President to discuss the China recovery and the threat from local competitors, especially EyeBright.

The discussion suggested that domestic competitors could gain meaningful unit share over the next two years. At the same time, the former executive emphasized that STAAR still has a significant moat through its proprietary Collamer technology, long clinical track record, and strong brand.

To me, none of these points change the long term thesis.

In fact, the business today is significantly stronger than it was when Alcon offered $30.75 per share near the bottom of the cycle. Since then, quarterly revenue has more than doubled from the depressed Q2 2025 level, yet the stock trades well below that offer.

I continue to believe STAAR is an attractive acquisition candidate. At today’s valuation, I think the risk reward is compelling.

As always, I never tell people when to sell a stock. When you’re up 100%, taking some profits is usually a reasonable decision. 

Comment: I doubled my position yesterday.

𝙏𝙝𝙚 𝘾𝙖𝙪𝙨𝙚 𝙔𝙤𝙪 𝙉𝙚𝙫𝙚𝙧 𝙎𝙖𝙬

One of the central insights of Buddhism is that every event arises from countless causes and conditions. Most of them are impossible for us to see, let alone predict.

Imagine this.

A cold front over the Pacific changes the jet stream. Three days later, Seoul has perfect weather.

Because the weather is so nice, Jung Min-ho, one of the leaders of the Korean Ants, decides to go clubbing instead of staying home. He gets home at 4 a.m., wakes up in a surprisingly good mood, and messages his friends:

“Today we buy on leverage.”

The Korean market closes up 5%.

Nasdaq futures follow another 1.5%.

A few hours later, Wall Street publishes detailed reports explaining that the rally was caused by macroeconomic data, interest rate expectations, and improving sentiment.

Maybe those explanations contain part of the truth. But maybe the chain of causes began with a change in the weather over the Pacific. Or perhaps even earlier, with causes nobody could ever identify.

The Buddhist point is not that events happen randomly. It’s that reality is shaped by an unimaginably vast web of causes and conditions. We see only a tiny fraction of that web, yet our minds confidently invent simple stories that make us feel as if we understand why things happened.

Marco Zappacosta (@mlz) went to school with my daughter Jesse.

His dad is Pierluigi Zappacosta, the founder of Logitech, so Marco grew up watching what it looks like to build something from scratch.

When he pitched me @thumbtack, I made him an offer almost on the spot because of the quiet confidence he wields. You can feel it in a room when someone genuinely sees a problem that shouldn't exist and has the energy and the intelligence to make it right.

His insight was simple: there was no real marketplace connecting the millions of plumbers, cleaners, movers, and contractors who power homes and offices with the people who actually need them. Every other marketplace was either too narrow or too broken.

He fixed that.

The company has redefined how homeowners and landlords do home improvement and repair.. The company is still private, still growing revenues, still processing over millions of customer service requests a year across millions of registered users.

That quiet confidence in the pitch room showed up in how he ran the company too. It looks like a great success.
